content.definition: Sinus rhythm is atrial activation from the sinoatrial node, usually with upright P waves in lead II, one P wave before each QRS and a consistent PR interval.
Sinus bradycardia is sinus rhythm below 60/min. It is dangerous when it causes hypotension, shock, ischaemia, altered mentation, low urine output or low cardiac output.
Sinus tachycardia is sinus rhythm above 100/min. In CTICU it is usually compensatory: pain, anxiety, fever, hypovolaemia, bleeding, sepsis, low cardiac output, tamponade, hypoxia or catecholamines.

content.text: The bedside question is whether the rate is the problem or the compensation. Suppressing sinus tachycardia before correcting bleeding, tamponade, hypoxia or low stroke volume can collapse cardiac output.

items: Confirm sinus P waves and stable PR interval.
Assess arterial waveform, MAP, pulse pressure, lactate, urine output and skin perfusion.
For bradycardia, check hypothermia, vagal stimulus, beta-blockers, amiodarone, dexmedetomidine, opioids, hyperkalaemia and inferior MI.
For tachycardia, search for pain, fever, hypovolaemia, bleeding, sepsis, hypoxia, low cardiac output and tamponade.
Escalate HR below 50/min or sustained above 120-130/min when associated with hypoperfusion.

content.text: Sinus rhythm criteria

Sinus rhythm has upright P waves in lead II, a P before every QRS, a QRS after every P, and a stable PR interval usually 120-200 ms. The QRS may be wide from bundle branch block or pacing, but atrial origin remains sinus if P-wave morphology and conduction are consistent.
Why sinus bradycardia occurs after cardiac surgery

Post-operative sinus bradycardia may result from hypothermia, high vagal tone, beta-blockers, amiodarone, dexmedetomidine, opioids, inferior MI/RCA ischaemia, sinus node dysfunction, hyperkalaemia, atrial surgery or oedema near conduction tissue.
When bradycardia is benign or dangerous

Sinus bradycardia may be benign when the patient is asleep, warm, perfusing, with stable MAP, good pulse pressure, urine output at least 0.5 mL/kg/hr and falling lactate. It is dangerous when stroke volume cannot rise enough to maintain CO, especially in LV dysfunction, RV failure, pulmonary hypertension, tamponade, vasoplegia or post-valve diastolic dysfunction.
Drug causes

rows: Slow SA node and AV node; reduce oxygen demand.
Useful but can worsen shock or AV block.
Can cause bradycardia, hypotension during IV loading and QT prolongation.
Can cause marked bradycardia, especially with vagal triggers.
Consider toxicity, renal function and drug interaction.

content.text: Inferior MI, RV involvement and pacing wires

Inferior MI can involve RCA supply to the sinus and AV nodes. If RV infarction is also present, bradycardia is harmful because RV output and LV preload are already low. Temporary epicardial wires should be checked early in valve, aortic and post-CABG patients at risk of bradycardia or AV block.
Sinus tachycardia as compensation

Sinus tachycardia is often the body's attempt to maintain CO when stroke volume is low. Causes include pain, anxiety, fever, shivering, hypovolaemia, bleeding, tamponade, sepsis, hypoxia, anaemia, PE, withdrawal, low cardiac output and inotrope exposure. Treat the cause before suppressing the rate.
Haemodynamic consequences

Bradycardia reduces CO when stroke volume cannot compensate. Tachycardia shortens diastole, reduces coronary perfusion, increases oxygen demand and reduces ventricular filling. Many post-cardiac surgery patients perform best around 70-90/min, but the target must be judged by perfusion, not a generic number.
Nursing actions

items: Print a rhythm strip or 12-lead if uncertain.
Check temperature, ABG, K, Mg, Ca, Hb, pain, sedation, fluid balance, chest drain output and vasoactive doses.
For compromised bradycardia, prepare atropine 1 mg IV, pacing and senior review.
For tachycardia, treat pain, fever, bleeding, hypovolaemia, sepsis, hypoxia, tamponade or low cardiac output before rate suppression.

content.text: Sinus tachycardia after cardiac surgery may be the first visible sign of bleeding or tamponade.
Dexmedetomidine plus beta-blockade plus suctioning is a classic bradycardia setup.
A lower HR after beta-blocker is not success unless MAP, lactate, urine output and symptoms improve.
Do not reflexively beta-block sinus tachycardia until bleeding, tamponade, sepsis, hypoxia and low output are considered.
ECG and monitor setup

For sinus bradycardia and tachycardia, the ECG monitor must be treated as a clinical device, not wallpaper. Use a lead that shows atrial activity when P waves matter, print strips during events, check electrode contact, and repeat a 12-lead when rhythm diagnosis, ischaemia or conduction disease is uncertain.
Arterial waveform correlation

The arterial line determines whether electrical activity is producing mechanical output. Rate, rhythm and monitor labels are secondary to MAP, pulse pressure, waveform amplitude, lactate, urine output and clinical perfusion.
Defibrillator and pacing readiness

Symptomatic bradycardia may require pacing; unstable tachycardia may reveal shock physiology requires early access to pads, synchronised cardioversion, defibrillation or pacing depending on stability. In post-cardiac surgery patients, also check epicardial wires, pacing box battery, output, sensitivity, capture and sensing.

rows: Lead quality, rhythm strip, alarm limits and artefact.
Avoids missed rhythm change or false diagnosis.
Conduction, QRS width, ischaemia and baseline comparison.
Clarifies rhythm and identifies dangerous mimics.
Level, zero, damping, pulse pressure and waveform presence.
Pads, sync markers, battery and energy pathway.
Mode, rate, output, sensitivity and capture threshold.
Essential when rate or AV synchrony fails.
Treat if hypoperfusion or high-risk cause exists.
Atropine, pacing readiness and senior review.
1 mg IV every 3-5 min to maximum 3 mg
Bridge for unstable bradycardia if needed.
Bridge for unstable bradycardia/shock with senior direction.
Sustained above 120-130/min with low pulse pressure, ischaemia or shock
Investigate bleeding, tamponade, sepsis, hypoxia and low output.

content.text: Escalate bradycardia below 50/min or tachycardia above 120-130/min with hypoperfusion.
Escalate urgently for bradycardia with high-grade block or tachycardia with suspected bleeding, tamponade, sepsis or LCOS.
Activate emergency response for peri-arrest bradycardia, shock, loss of arterial waveform or VT/VF.

quiz.question: Why can suppressing sinus tachycardia after cardiac surgery be harmful?
options: It may be maintaining cardiac output when stroke volume is low.
quiz.explanation: If stroke volume is low, HR may preserve CO; suppressing it can collapse perfusion.

quiz.question: Which bradycardia pattern is dangerous?
options: HR 42/min with hypotension and rising lactate.
quiz.explanation: Bradycardia with hypoperfusion is symptomatic and needs urgent management.

quiz.question: What drug combination commonly contributes to bradycardia?
options: Dexmedetomidine plus beta-blockade plus vagal stimulation.
quiz.explanation: Sympatholysis plus AV nodal slowing and vagal stimulus can produce marked bradycardia.
